content.full_text:
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 24,
1992
Norm Waters
6250 Old Sacramento Road
Plymouth,
CA
95669
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-346
Dear Mr. Waters:
This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of
advice I provided to you on June 16,
1992, regarding your
responsibilities under the “personal use” provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).’
Effective January 1,
1990,
the
Act was amended to include new provisions which regulate the
appropriate use of campaign funds.
(Section 89510,
The general rule of the new personal use law is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must, at a minimum,
be reasonably
related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose
associated with the candidate’s office.
2
(Section 89512.)
Surplus campaign funds are subject to further limitation on
their use.
Pursuant to the definition of “surplus funds” in
Section 89519,
your campaign funds became surplus campaign funds
at the end of the post-election reporting period following your
defeat in the 1990 election for State Assembly.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
However,
where an expenditure confers a substantial personal
benefit on the candidate, the expenditure must be directly related
to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section
89512.)
A “substantial personal benefit” is defined as a direct
personal benefit to the candidate of $100 or more.
(Section
89511(b) (2).)
California
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •
(916)322-5660


Our File No. A-92-346
Page
2
Thus, your surplus campaign funds may only be expended for
the following:
(a)
The payment of outstanding campaign debts
or elected officer’s expenses.
(b)
The pro-rata repayment of contributions.
(c)
Donations to any bona fide charitable,
educational, civic, religious,
or similar tax—
exempt, nonprofit organization, where no
substantial part of the proceeds will have a
material financial effect on the former candidate
or elected officer,
any member of his or her
immediate family,
or his or her campaign treasurer.
(d)
Contributions to a political party or
committee so long as the funds are not used to make
contributions in support of or opposition to a
candidate for elective office.
(e)
Contributions to support or oppose any
candidate for federal office,
any candidate for
elective office in a state other than California,
or any ballot measure.
(f)
The payment for professional services
reasonably required by the committee to assist in
the performance of its administrative functions,
including payment for attorney’s fees for
litigation which arises directly out of a
candidate’s or elected officer’s activities,
duties,
or status as a candidate or elected
officer,
including, but not limited to,
an action
to enjoin defamation,
defense of an action brought
for a violation of state or local campaign,
disclosure or election laws,
and an action arising
from an election contest or recount.
Section 89519.
Outstanding Campaign Debts
Payments received by a candidate or officeholder for the
purpose of paying attorney fees and fines incurred in connection
with the candidate’s or officer’s official duties have generally
been considered contributions.
(See,
In re Johnson
(1989)
12
FPPC
Ops.
1;
Brand Advice Letter,
No.
1—90-106.)
Consistent with this
line of authority, the personal use provisions of the Act permit
the use of campaign funds to pay attorney fees and other costs in
connection with administrative litigation where the litigation is
directly related to the candidate’s activities, duties,
or status
as a candidate or elected officer.
(Section 89514.)
Fines may


Our File No. A-92-346
Page
3
also be paid, under the same circumstances, with campaign funds.
(Section 89513(c) (2);
Mayo Advice Letter,
T-90-684.)
Surplus campaign funds may also be used for this purpose if
the violation was in connection with the election for which the
funds were initially raised.
In 1991,
Section 89519 was amended
to expressly permit the payment of attorney’s fees for litigation
which arises directly out of a candidate’s or elected officer’s
activities, duties,
or status as a candidate or elected officer.
Administrative fines imposed on an officer which arise out of the
litigation would be considered outstanding campaign debts which
could be paid with surplus campaign funds.
(Section 89519(a);
See
also,
Section 89519(f).)
Donations
Section 89519 permits surplus campaign funds
3 to be used to
make donations to nonprofit organizations if both of the following
apply:
(1)
the recipient organization is a bona fide charitable,
educational, civic, religious,
or similar tax—exempt nonprofit
organization;
and,
(2)
no substantial part of the proceeds of the
donation will have a material financial effect on you or any
member of your immediate family.
(Section 89519(c).)
We have
advised that surplus campaign funds may be provided to
governmental agencies,
such as fire departments, consistent with
Section 89519(c), under the circumstances set forth above.
(See
generally, Jasper Advice Letter,
No.
A-90—5l1.)
Please note that the Commission will be considering
regulations that interpret the “personal use” provisions of the
Act in the future.
Should new regulations on these issues be
adopted, this advice may change.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
cpp
ohn W. Wallace
el, Legal Division
SH:JWW:aa
“Campaign funds” are defined as contributions, cash,
cash
equivalents,
and other assets of the committee.
(Section
89511(b)(1).)

sections.question_synthetic: What are the permissible uses of surplus campaign funds under California's personal use provisions of the Political Reform Act, specifically following a candidate's defeat in a 1990 State Assembly election?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Surplus campaign funds may only be expended for: (1) payment of outstanding campaign debts or elected officer expenses; (2) pro-rata repayment of contributions; (3) donations to tax-exempt nonprofit organizations with no material benefit to the candidate or immediate family; (4) contributions to political parties or committees (not for candidate support/opposition); (5) contributions to federal candidates, out-of-state candidates, or ballot measures; and (6) professional services and attorney fees directly related to the candidate's status or activities, including litigation and administrative fines.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document structured as FPPC response to requestor's inquiry for confirmation of prior oral advice. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present, but content clearly contains the legal question and FPPC's detailed answer regarding surplus campaign fund usage. Document provides extensive statutory citations and regulatory guidance throughout.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the permissible uses of surplus campaign funds under California's personal use provisions of the Political Reform Act, specifically following a candidate's defeat in a 1990 State Assembly election?

CONCLUSION: Surplus campaign funds may only be expended for: (1) payment of outstanding campaign debts or elected officer expenses; (2) pro-rata repayment of contributions; (3) donations to tax-exempt nonprofit organizations with no material benefit to the candidate or immediate family; (4) contributions to political parties or committees (not for candidate support/opposition); (5) contributions to federal candidates, out-of-state candidates, or ballot measures; and (6) professional services and attorney fees directly related to the candidate's status or activities, including litigation and administrative fines.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms advice provided to Norm Waters regarding the permissible uses of his surplus campaign funds following his defeat in the 1990 State Assembly election, detailing six specific categories of allowed expenditures under the Political Reform Act's personal use provisions.
